preFix.title: Repairing a rotted wooden gate post
preFix.subtitle: Stabilise and rebuild a soft or crumbling gate post section with epoxy consolidant and filler
preFix.excerpt: How to repair a gate post that has developed wet rot at or near ground level, using epoxy wood consolidant to stabilise soft timber and two-part epoxy filler to rebuild the missing section, without digging out and replacing the post.
preFix.sourceNotes: BRE Good Repair Guide 13 (wood rot in buildings). PCA (Property Care Association) guidance on wet rot. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: Wet rot at the base of a gate post is common where the post is in contact with soil or where water pools against the wood. The repair described here is suitable when the post is still structurally sound in its upper section and the rot is confined to one face or corner at low level. If the post is rotten through its full cross-section at ground level it must be replaced. For partial rot,
followed by a two-part epoxy filler gives a repair that will outlast the surrounding timber.

Two-part epoxy wood consolidant. Two-part epoxy wood filler (sold in tubes or tubs; Ronseal High Performance Wood Filler and similar products are suitable for exterior use). Wood preservative. Exterior primer and paint or opaque wood stain to finish. Timber battens or Plasteline modelling clay to form mould edges around cavities.
Step 1, expose and remove all rotten timber

Scrape and dig out all soft, spongy, or brown-friable timber with a chisel or screwdriver. Continue until you reach firm, sound wood on all sides of the repair area. Cut back any split or crumbling edges squarely with the tenon saw. Brush out all loose debris. Allow the exposed timber to dry thoroughly before proceeding; applying consolidant to wet wood reduces its penetration significantly.

Mix the two parts of the consolidant according to the manufacturer's ratio and brush it generously onto the exposed repair area. Drill several 6 mm holes into the timber at the repair zone and pour consolidant into the holes as well. Allow the first application to soak in and repeat two or three times until the surface no longer absorbs more liquid. The consolidant should penetrate 10 to 20 mm into sound wood. Allow to cure fully before filling.

Mix the two-part epoxy filler and press it firmly into the repair cavity, working in layers no deeper than 20 mm to avoid shrinkage cracking. Use Plasteline or timber battens temporarily fixed alongside the repair to form the outer face of deep cavities. Build the filler proud of the surrounding surface by 2 to 3 mm. Allow to cure. Sand back to the original profile with the sanding block, working through 80, 120, and 180 grit in sequence.

Apply exterior wood primer to all bare and repaired surfaces, then topcoat with two coats of exterior paint or opaque stain. Seal the base of the post where it meets the soil with a preservative paste or by fitting a post boot to keep moisture away from the repaired zone.

attrs.fix: Remove all loose filler. Re-apply consolidant and allow it to cure for at least 48 hours in dry conditions. Re-fill.
attrs.cause: Consolidant was not applied or had not fully cured before the filler was applied, so the filler is bonding to soft wood rather than hardened substrate.
attrs.symptom: Filler pulls away from the repair within a few months

attrs.fix: Fit a steel post base or a post boot to lift the timber clear of the soil. Improve drainage around the post base. The repair itself is sound; the problem is a recurring moisture source.
attrs.cause: The moisture source was not eliminated — soil is still in contact with the post, or water is pooling at the base.
attrs.symptom: Rot returns at the same location after two to three years

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
